title: Observation antibunching with classical light in a linear interferometer
authors: Yu Gu, Yuhan Ma, Yiqi Song, Meixue Chen, Hui Chen, Huaibin Zheng, Yuchen He, Yu Zhou, Fuli Li, Zhuo Xu, Jianbin Liu
abstract: Understanding the boundary between classical and nonclassical phenomena is important for both fundamental researches in quantum optics and applications in quantum information. One of the most interesting research directions in this field is exploring nonclassical effects with classical light. In this paper, we will show that it is possible to observe antibunching with thermal light in a Hanbury Brown-Twiss interferometer by treating single-photon detectors as photon-number-resolving detectors to perform photon-number projection measurements. Both temporal and spatial antibunching is observed via the correlation of two detectors detecting one and zero photon, respectively. By comparing the measured results of thermal and laser light, it is found that the observed antibunching arises from the combined effect of photon statistics of thermal light and photon-number projection measurement.The classical and nonclassical nature of the observed antibunching is analyzed. The results are helpful to understand the connection between classical and nonclassical correlation and may find applications in multiphoton interference and quantum imaging.
summary: The paper demonstrates that antibunching, typically a nonclassical phenomenon, can be observed using classical thermal light in a Hanbury Brown-Twiss interferometer. This is achieved by using single-photon detectors to perform photon-number projection measurements. The findings help clarify the boundary between classical and quantum correlations in optical systems.
main_problem: Exploring the boundary between classical and nonclassical phenomena by investigating whether antibunching effects can be observed using classical thermal light.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated that antibunching can be observed with thermal light in a Hanbury Brown-Twiss interferometer by utilizing single-photon detectors as photon-number-resolving detectors for photon-number projection measurements.
method: The study uses a Hanbury Brown-Twiss interferometer setup and treats single-photon detectors as photon-number-resolving detectors to perform photon-number projection measurements on thermal and laser light.
model_or_system: A linear interferometer setup (Hanbury Brown-Twiss type) using thermal light and laser light as the input optical states.
key_observables: Temporal and spatial antibunching via the correlation of two detectors detecting one and zero photons, respectively.
important_parameters: Photon statistics of thermal light and photon-number projection measurement settings.
main_assumptions: The observation relies on the ability to treat single-photon detectors as photon-number-resolving detectors through projection measurements.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the research motivation regarding classical vs nonclassical boundaries, describes the experimental demonstration of antibunching with thermal light, compares thermal and laser light results, and analyzes the classical and nonclassical nature of the observed effects.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant to quantum optics as it explores the fundamental connection between classical and nonclassical correlations and demonstrates how measurement techniques can reveal nonclassical-like signatures in classical light.
